mnemonics.title: Taylor Series Order Memory
mnemonics.content:
ZEROTH: Just the point value (f(a))
FIRST: Add the slope × distance (∇f × Δx)
SECOND: Add half the curve (½ΔxᵀHΔx)

mnemonics.title: Jacobian and Hessian Memory
mnemonics.content:
JACK the First: Jacobian = First derivatives
HARRY the Second: Hessian = Second derivatives

acronyms.usage: Bounds error in Taylor approximation: Rₙ = O(||x-a||ⁿ⁺¹)
Second-order partial derivatives; determines curvature for optimization
First-order partial derivatives; generalization of derivative to vector functions
Optimization problems where Taylor series is applied to nonlinear objective/constraints

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a curved surface z = f(x,y). The tangent plane at (a,b,f(a,b)) touches the surface at one point and approximates it locally - this is linearization in 3D.
Think of Hessian as capturing 'bending' of function. Positive definite = bowl-shaped (minimum), negative definite = inverted bowl (maximum), indefinite = saddle-shaped.
Draw concentric circles around expansion point - accuracy decreases outward. Linear is good nearby, higher orders extend accurate region.

analogies.analogy: Like using a straight ruler to measure a curved line - accurate only over short distances, but simple and useful.
Like zoomed-in map: at large scale you see curves, but as you zoom in (Taylor expansion), the terrain appears increasingly linear.
Like a car's suspension: stiff (positive definite) = bottom of valley (minimum), soft (negative definite) = top of hill (maximum), mixed = twisty road (saddle).
